groups.context_description: Prophetic boldness — inner audacity to declare what runs counter to expectation or convention, grounded in divine revelation
Inner quality of confident boldness in apostolic communication — willingness to speak directly and without inhibition, grounded in divine grace
Presumptuous boldness — inner disposition of audacious arrogance that displaces fitting reverence, showing no inner trembling before what is holy
Boldness as inner posture of access to God — confident approach to God's presence made possible through Christ, expressed in prayer, worship, and hope of judgment
Boldness as inner quality of apostolic proclamation — confident unrestrained declaration of the gospel grounded in encounter with Christ and sustained through hope
Boldness as inner quality of relational confidence — freedom to speak openly and without self-protective restraint in significant relationships
Bold proclamation as inner-being posture — speaking with unrestrained confidence in the face of opposition and risk, grounded in God
Inner-being self-direction: yielding, restraining, consecrating, or pledging the self before God or others
Moral accountability and covenantal claim
Measure of inner-being excellence
The monument as lasting inner-being identity and legacy
Divine presence as spacious refuge
Human purposeful undertaking as the site of divine blessing and inner-being rejoicing
Sole of foot as marker of total inner-being suffering and restlessness
Foot as marker of divine presence, submission, and holiness
Hardening of the heart — wilful inner resistance to God
'Be strong and courageous' — divine or prophetic command to inner courage grounded in divine presence
Strengthening the weak — care for the inner being of another
Self-strengthening in God — inner act of drawing on divine strength in crisis or mission
Strengthening that enables or reflects a moral orientation — toward good or ill
Holding fast to integrity, wisdom, or covenant
God holding the person — divine grasp as reassurance and covenant
Being seized by inner anguish, fear, or grief
The inner-being act of grasping — idols or false support vs God
Inner resolve expressed as firm insistence or determined compliance
Prevailing through inner strength or resolve
Inner character in contest — resolute or irresolute, audacious or guile-driven
Perseverance as inner-being posture — standing firm despite pressure, grounded in resolve or knowledge of God
Force as coercive domination — inner disposition of aggression and self-willed domination
Force as urgent intensity in prayer and repentance
Comparative growth of strength — prevailing over opposition
Strength as an inner quality — stoutness of heart, or strength that commands inner awe
Inner strength as an inadequate resource before divine judgment
The prophet as vessel of the divine word — inner-being vocation
The false prophet — inner-being failure of self-generated speech, greed, and deception
Rejection of the prophet — Israel's inner-being resistance to God's claim through prophetic calling
Bold inner action in service of mission or truth
Inner discipline of restraint — not presuming beyond what is appropriate
Divine address of inner courage in personal need, fear, or healing
Divine address of inner courage for mission and endurance under tribulation
Term names the inner condition of distraction — the person whose inner orientation is pulled and scattered by competing demands; the divided attention that displaces devotion
Term names the inner quality of soundness and wholeness of orientation — the undivided inner focus that allows the whole person to be full of light
Term names the inner disposition of unreserved giving — generosity without holding back or finding fault; the quality of openhanded-ness as inner character
Term names the inner condition of being undistracted — the whole person directed toward the Lord without division or competing claim on attention
Term names the inner quality of singleness and sincerity — the undivided, transparent orientation of the heart toward God and others; the opposite of duplicity or divided allegiance
Term names the inner disposition of generosity — the openhanded orientation of the person that overflows in giving; giving as the expression of inner singleness
Perpetual inner moral condition — chronic spiritual backsliding without return
Distinguished inner excellence — the excellent spirit as the ground of outward distinction
Lament over God's apparent perpetual absence — inner anguish of "how long?"
Perpetual inner suffering and unceasing pain — anguish that will not relent
Everlasting joy, hope and divine faithfulness — inner orientation toward what endures

Term names the doing of good as the inner-being disposition and practice that characterises those who belong to God — expressed in love for enemies, endurance through unjust suffering, and righteous conduct before others

Term names the doing of good as an inner-being orientation — both as God's generous provision that satisfies the human heart, and as the inner disposition of generosity that believers are commanded to embody
Term names doing good as the ongoing inner-being commitment maintained even in suffering — the practice that accompanies the entrusting of the soul to God
Term names the person characterised by doing good — a moral-character designation that reflects an inner orientation visible in conduct and recognised by others
Term names the inner disposition of not loving good — an inner-being orientation that characterises the person given over to vice, as part of a catalogue of moral darkness
Term names goodness as the exclusive and defining attribute of God's inner being — the standard by which all human goodness is measured and found derivative
Term names goodness as the inner character quality of the person — the good heart, good conscience, good treasure — from which good action necessarily flows
Term names goodness as the inner moral orientation and activity that God commands and will judge — doing good, pursuing good, overcoming evil with good
Term names goodness as the eschatological and providential good that God works for and toward — the good things to come, good hope, all things working for good
Term names the inner conflict between the person's desire for good and their inability to perform it — the good I want but cannot do, the good that exposes the power of sin in the inner person
Term names the experience of being afflicted or oppressed — the inner suffering, loss of dignity, and vulnerability of those under the power of another person or system
Term names God's deliberate humbling of a person or people — the inner work of affliction that tests what is in the heart, produces dependence on God, and results in learning and spiritual growth
Term names the inner act of self-humbling before God — the voluntary afflicting of oneself through fasting, penitence, and prostration as the expression of inward submission and dependence
Term names the inner experience of the afflicted person — the condition of deep suffering through which faith is tested, reoriented, and strengthened toward God
Term names fasting as the physical expression of an inner-being posture of grief, penitence, and humble submission before God

Term names the inner disposition of gentleness and reasonableness — a character quality that avoids quarrelling and force, and yields to what is fair, as required of leaders and displayed in wisdom from above
Term names meekness/gentleness as an inner-being disposition of the renewed person — the strength that yields without force, operating in correction, conflict, and community as fruit of the Spirit
Term names the inner disposition of gracious, reasonable fairness — the gentleness that is willing to yield to the reasonable rather than insisting on the strict letter; modelled by Christ
Term names the humble/meek person — one whose inner disposition is characterised by lowliness, teachableness, and openness toward God
Term names the poor/afflicted person — one whose inner experience is shaped by material need, oppression, and dependence on God for vindication
Term names gentleness/condescension as an inner quality — either God's toward the person, or the king's inner disposition of meekness in the exercise of power
Term names humility/meekness as an inner quality — the disposition of the person before God and others that precedes honour and is rewarded by God
Term names humility as the inner condition accompanying wisdom — the disposition of the person who, avoiding pride, gains understanding
Term names the action of bringing low — God humbling the proud, pride being abased, or the person being made low in their inner standing before God and others
Term names the inner disposition of lowliness — the lowly spirit as the inner quality that contrasts with pride and is favoured by God
Term names the inner disposition of humble walking with God — the ongoing orientation of the whole person toward God in lowliness
Term names the inner act of humbling oneself — the voluntary lowering of self-estimation before God, which results in exaltation
Term names the state of being brought low — whether by God's action, by humbling circumstances, or through learned contentment in hardship
Term names the state of lowliness or humiliation — the condition of being in a lowly estate, whether as a quality God regards, a suffering Christ bore, or the passing nature of worldly status
Term names gentleness/meekness as an inner quality of the person — a settled inner disposition of mildness and strength-under-control, shown in how one bears oneself toward God and others
Term names gentleness as an inner quality — the disposition that governs the reception of God's word, the exercise of wisdom, and engagement with others
Term names lowliness/humility as an inner quality of the person — a settled inner disposition of not elevating oneself, which God values and responds to with grace and exaltation
Term names humility as an inner quality of the renewed person — the disposition that re-orients self-estimation, governs communal relations, and characterises service to God
Term names false or counterfeit humility — the outward performance of self-abasement without genuine inner transformation
Term names the inner act of waiting with hope — the expectant orientation of the soul toward God's action, help, or timing
Term names inner weariness — the exhaustion of the inner person (or divine person) through sustained effort, trial, or moral burden
Term names the hardship endured on the way — the suffering borne by a people under God's leadership or under judgment, engaging their inner capacity for perseverance
Term names extended duration that shapes inner orientation — length of time pressing upon the inner life of those who wait or endure
Term names patient forbearance as an inner disposition — the willingness to wait, endure, or bear with others without giving way to frustration
Term names divine patience — God's inner disposition of forbearance toward human disobedience
Term names longsuffering/patience as an inner character quality and fruit of the Spirit — the sustained inner disposition of forbearance toward others and circumstances
Term names divine longsuffering — God's patient endurance of human sin, restraining judgment to extend the opportunity for repentance
Term names steadfast endurance under trial — the inner disposition that holds firm under pressure, suffering, or persecution
Term names perseverance as the sustained inner disposition of steadfast endurance under trial — producing character, hope, and ultimately eternal life
Term names the prolonging of life as covenantal promise — obedience to God's commands linked to length of days as the inner-being fruit of faithfulness
Term names the inner disposition of patience or anger-restraint — slowness as a character quality prolonging relationship and possibility
Term names slowness to anger as a divine and human inner disposition — the character of patient restraint that holds wrath in check and sustains relationship
Term names length of days as covenantal gift and inner aspiration — the divine promise of a long life as the fruit of wisdom, faithfulness, and God's favour
Term names self-control as an inner character quality — the capacity to govern oneself, listed as a fruit of the Spirit and a virtue of mature character
Term names the exercise of self-control as an inner capacity — governing appetite and desire in both relational and competitive contexts
Term names sound-mindedness as an inner quality — the rational, morally disciplined disposition of the person, whether in speech or conduct
groups.notes: Anchored — dimension analytically unambiguous. C01 anchor session 2026-04-06.

groups.context_description: Term names absence of inner restraint as vulnerability — a person without self-control is exposed like a city without walls
Term names the absence of inner-being qualities — no fear of God, no understanding, no comforter, no shepherd, no hope — where negation discloses an inner-being deficit or moral absence
Term names lack of self-control as an inner vulnerability — the absence of inner restraint that creates exposure to temptation
Term names self-discipline as an inner gift of the Spirit — the quality of a sound and controlled inner life given by God, in contrast to a spirit of fear
Term names intemperance as an inner character vice — the absence of inner governance listed among qualities of moral deterioration
Term names self-controlled as an inner character requirement — among the virtues required of one who leads others
Term names inner restraint — the holding back or releasing of inner content (prophetic compulsion, honest speech, moral response) where restraint or its absence is at stake
Term names divine restraint of human capacity — shutting the womb, stopping strength, withholding rain — as divine sovereignty over inner human destiny and the conditions of life

Term names the solemn assembly as a collective inner-being occasion — the community gathered for worship and covenant renewal, where the inner moral state of participants is the decisive factor for its validity
Term names coercive oppression and restraint — where oppression diminishes the inner life, the barren womb expresses insatiable inner longing, and the servant suffers unjust restraint
Term names absence of restraint as the ground for inner confidence — nothing can hinder God from saving, which grounds Jonathan's bold trust
Term names the restraining authority as the social structure enforcing inner moral accountability — its absence leaves the community without external shaming restraint
